text: Thirty-two-year-old female who had native medicine-induced diarrhea suffered from weight loss and recurrent episodes of vomiting. On evaluation, SMA syndrome was identified.
Sagittal section CT shows decreased iotomicentric angle.

The cut sections reveal a decreased space between the aorta and superior mesenteric artery (SMA), along with a visibly distended stomach extending from the third part of the duodenum (DIII) to the junction between DIII and the fourth part of the duodenum (DIV).
The surgeon initiates a laparoscopic duodenal jejunal bypass procedure, preparing to create a bypass between the duodenum and jejunum.
A FIMM scope is introduced through the subumbilical position, providing a visual check of the surgical field.
The surgeon removes adhesions in the suprapubic region and introduces a TNMM port into the suprapubic space, followed by the insertion of a camera through the port for visualization.
The laparoscopic camera provides a clear view of the infracolic compartment, including the third and fourth parts of the duodenum (DIII and DIV).
The surgeon demonstrates the DIII segment of the duodenum, the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), and the pancreas. The duodenum is being cauterized, showing the application of heat to the tissue.

The surgeon retracts the colon upwards and holds a leaf of mesocolon, pulling the right colic artery downwards. The duodenum is mobilized through the transmesocolic route between the middle colic and right colic arteries. The mesocolon leaf is dissected, and the duodenum is caucarized.
The surgeon performs dissection using a harmonic scalpel, likely focusing on the previously exposed anatomical structures such as the duodenum and mesocolon.
The surgical team carefully navigates around the right colic artery, middle colic artery, and the marginal arcade of Drummond, ensuring these vascular structures remain intact during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to separate the mesocolon from the duodenum, followed by detaching the duodenum from its retroperitoneal attachments, completing the caucarization process.
So this caucarization is necessary to mobilize

The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum at the DII and DIII levels, ensuring sufficient length for a tension-free anastomosis with the jejunum, located five to ten centimeters distal to the DJ flexure. The retroperitoneal attachments of the duodenum are taken down, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) below.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the final portion of the D-III segment of the duodenum, ensuring it is free from retroperitoneal attachments.
The surgeon traces the jejunum starting from the duodenal jejunal flexure, following its path distally.
A suitable loop of jejunum is brought above the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV), positioned close to the mobilized sections of the duodenum (D-II and D-III).
The surgeon performs a double-layered hand-sewn continuous anastomosis between the jejunum and the mobilized duodenum, ensuring a secure connection.

The surgeon performs the outer layer of the anastomosis using a 3-0 Prolene continuous suture, carefully rotating the duodenum and taking the first bite from the posterior aspect to prevent narrowing. Similarly, the jejunum is sutured close to the mesentery to maintain lumen width.
The surgeon takes the first bite of the suture and carefully places a knot to tighten and square it, ensuring secure closure of the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses the smaller side of the suture as retraction to clearly visualize the length of the D-II and D-III segments and the jejunal loop, preparing these structures for anastomosis.
The surgeon brings both the jejunal and duodenal loops close together by retracting them, preparing for further anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the fourth layer of the continuous hand-sewn anastomosis using proline suture, ensuring the integrity of the jejunostomy without narrowing the duodenum or jejunum.
The advantage of proline is we can take series of bites and then pull it.
The surgeon tightens the proline suture, which remains secure without the need for additional tension between bites, unlike braided sutures such as vicryl or silk.
care is taken to avoid injury to the mesenteric vasculature.
As we discussed, since it's proline, we can pull the sutures after putting two or three bites.
Again, care is taken to avoid too much narrowing.
The surgeon takes sutures from the posterior aspect of the duodenum, ensuring precise placement close to the tissue.

The surgeon opens the duodenum and jejunum using a harmonic scalpel, creating incisions approximately six to seven centimeters in length. Care is taken to avoid posterior wall injury and thermal damage by rotating the harmonic scalpel during the procedure.
So, the inactive portion was within the lumen.
The surgeon performs a continuous anastomosis of the second and third layers using vicryl sutures, ensuring secure connection between the duodenum and jejunum.
The surgeon places the first vicryl suture at the inferior corner of the anastomosis site, using it to provide retraction for better visualization and access.
A proline suture is placed superiorly for retraction during the jejunostomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a vicryl suture inferiorly to secure the jejunum, contributing to the retraction and stabilization of the tissue during the jejunostomy procedure.
The surgeon places a corner suture to secure the junction and prevent missing the corner during the anastomosis, ensuring precise alignment from above to below.

The surgeon cuts the corner suture and begins suturing from above to below, using a technique similar to the proline first layer. Cornell sutures are placed in an in-out pattern on the contralateral loop of the jejunum, and then tied. The needle is prepared for the next step.

The surgeon introduces the needle from outside to inside at the corner of the jejunum, retracting the anterior wall. The posterior wall is held and retracted by pulling the suture, allowing for common bites to be taken between the duodenum and jejunum. A series of such bites are performed to secure the connection.

The surgeon uses vicryl sutures in a continuous manner from cephalic to caudal direction, tightening each bite individually. The suture is then tied to the initial corner suture of vicryl previously placed.

The surgeon prepares to bring the vicryl suture from above downwards for the third layer, carefully taking bites to invert the mucosa properly. The technique involves taking zero submucosal sutures or minimal mucosal bites.
The surgeon carefully inverts the mucosa of the jejunum to avoid eversion during suturing, ensuring proper alignment of the tissue layers.
And this is more visible in laparoscopy than in open.
The advantage of PDS is, as I said earlier, after taking two or three bites, one can pull it, but the disadvantage is its memory.
So in between two or three bites, pulling it may become slightly cumbersome, but as we get experienced, it becomes easy.
The surgeon ties the corner suture to the posterior layer suture, completing the inner layer sutures of the duodenal vaginal bypass.
So here care is taken to avoid any gap in the angle particularly.
The surgeon places interrupted sutures to close any gaps between the previously placed sutures, ensuring a continuous closure of the surgical site.

The surgeon completes the final layer of the anastomosis by running continuous Prolene sutures from below upwards, tying the Prolene with the initial knot from the first layer. The use of Prolene monofilament sutures allows for easier handling, with the ability to pull after taking two or three bites.
The surgeon completes the jejunostomy procedure by fully burying the anastomosis, ensuring that the sutured connection between the intestinal segments is not exposed.
The surgeon completes the final layer of the anastomosis, ensuring the sutures are securely placed to finalize the jejunostomy procedure.
The surgeon ensures hemostasis in the surgical field, using suction to remove any excess blood or fluids, maintaining a clear view of the operative area.
The surgical field reveals minimal subcutaneous fat, allowing clear visibility of the underlying vessels in the thin patient.
The surgeon places a drain near the completed anastomosis site, ensuring proper postoperative fluid management.
Patient was discharged on post-op day four following adequate oral intake and no vomiting.
Thank you.
